comment.body: We never go long or short in big size until a move is confirmed. Only once the trend is proven do we cut the weight of hedges. This is how anyone who prioritizes risk minimization operates. The goal isn’t to be right or to say you called it in advance, it’s to preserve your capital and grow it steadily. And the slope of that growth should be as steep as possible while having the lowest possible risk

comment.body:
1) he didn't read the tuesday intraday post were i reacted to the monday momentum, saying everyone must take Trump's tweet serious, even if it doesn't necessary mean that the market will moonshot, I attached two charts to this too.
also I said, that bcs spot was squeezed out of the range, the upward momentum for the week was confirmed... the right-tail that the market was betting on ;)) did it hit? yes, it did hit the target. 
market never moves in straight lines. bullish momentum was called on sunday, i called it bulltrap for several reasons that i explained (so you know, what would disprove this bias), and i was also occuped with re-explaining over and over again during the week that bulltrap doesn't mean to go short... it means nothing for a daytrader, it is only a warning for traders who trade in a longer timeframe... but some still not went through this point...

comment.body: PLTR has an unbeatable moat in terms of defense and government using their data analytics platform. C3 does not have that. It does have tremendous name recognition due to its ticker being AI. PLTR will go much higher long term given that govt will be using them to construct the digital eye in the sky etc.  If we are truly looking for quantum computing names there are better names out there compared to C3 such as IONQ and QBTS. Think of Dwave as the palantir of quantum computing.

comment.body:
to answer to this delulu things in short: 
analysis is not a "how is right more times" competition.
it is about mapping the probabilities, the potential malign and beningn pivots and their impacts/result, that clients can use to build strategies...

this obsession about "hit rate" is a typical disorder among low quality fintwitters, bcs any trader who earned stable growth (like myself) in real life, knows that the thing that matters is risk management: determine the risk and manage it (either through hedging actions, stop-losses, position shifts, or simply just to stay in cash and wait, etc.)

you can have a 35% hit rate, and still be successful.
simply because if you rigorously lose only 10 pts all the time, it is like 65*10 = 650 pts loss over 100 trades.
If you win only 19 pts on the remaining 35 trades, you are up with 15 pts already. 
ok?
schematic example, but hopefully you understand the idea... and IRL you would not lose 10 pts all the time (bcs most of the times you are out earlier), and you don't win only 15 pts all the time, bcs you size up the winners more... so... yeah

risk management and plan is what makes you a good trader, not the hit rate.
anybody who comes with it, is a rookie and unexperienced, or even a fake-trader

comment.body:
now the read on the markets is simple:
- market is complacent about disinflation and sept rate cuts. It is like 90% sure that the Fed will cut rates.
- they sell puts and buy calls, bidding up the upside vol, and creating a fat right tail.
is the market right about CPI and PPi datas?
I don't know, and nobody knows. We don't know the numbers.

we all know that what would be right, but it is a manipulated game full of political interest. 
we all know that there is no disinflation, and when the market starts to go up after a cut, it would risk even hyperinflation for the next 3-4 years.
but Trump decided to manage the debt. he doesn't care about inflation.
